The significant decrease in absorption for glucose and galactose when sodium is replaced by lithium directly demonstrates sodium-dependent cotransport, which is a form of indirect (secondary) active transport specifically categorized under sodium-dependent glucose cotransporters.

This indicates that their transport across the epithelial membrane is a sodium-dependent process (co-transport). In contrast, the rate for fructose remains virtually unchanged, indicating its absorption occurs via facilitated diffusion, which is independent of the sodium gradient.

labeledSubSubTopics.title: B3.2.7 Transport of water from roots to leaves during transpiration

labeledSubSubTopics.path: A - Unity and Diversity > A1.1 Water > A1.1.3 Cohesion of water molecules due to hydrogen bonding and consequences for organisms
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A1.1.3 Cohesion of water molecules due to hydrogen bonding and consequences for organisms
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Cohesion is a physical property of water due to hydrogen bonding; discussing its importance draws on water’s cohesive properties and their consequences for organisms.

parts.content: Discuss the importance of cohesion in transpiration in plants.
parts.markscheme:
- Cohesion (attraction between water molecules) keeps an unbroken column of water in the xylem. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Transpiration from leaves creates tension (negative pressure) and, because water molecules cohere, this pull is transmitted down the xylem to draw water up from the roots. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Cohesion helps water move against gravity and reduces the likelihood of the water column breaking (cavitation/embolism). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Cohesion is due to hydrogen bonding between water molecules. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- In cohesion–tension theory, cohesive forces help maintain mass flow of water in xylem as water evaporates from mesophyll/stomata. <marks>1 mark</marks>

The following table provides data on the pressures acting at the arterial and venous ends of a systemic capillary in a resting muscle tissue.

| Location in capillary | Hydrostatic pressure of blood / kPa | Osmotic pressure of plasma / kPa |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| Arterial end | 4.4 | 2.9 |
| Venous end | 1.2 | 2.9 |

Which statement correctly describes the net movement of fluid?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: B - Form and Function > B3.2 Transport > B3.2.11 Release and reuptake of tissue fluid in capillaries (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: B3.2.11 Release and reuptake of tissue fluid in capillaries (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question uses hydrostatic and osmotic (oncotic) pressures at arterial vs venous ends of a systemic capillary to determine net filtration vs reabsorption—i.e., Starling forces and tissue fluid movement in capillaries.

options.content: Arterial end: net pressure 1.5 kPa, promoting filtration.
options.markscheme: Net filtration pressure = hydrostatic pressure − osmotic pressure = $4.4 - 2.9 = 1.5\text{ kPa}$, so fluid moves out of the capillary (filtration) at the arterial end.

options.content: Arterial end: net pressure 7.3 kPa, promoting reabsorption.
options.markscheme: Incorrect. The net pressure is not $7.3\text{ kPa}$. Using the given values: $4.4 - 2.9 = 1.5\text{ kPa}$, and this favours filtration, not reabsorption.

options.content: Venous end: net pressure 1.2 kPa, promoting filtration.
options.markscheme: Incorrect. Net pressure at the venous end is $1.2 - 2.9 = -1.7\text{ kPa}$, which favours reabsorption, not filtration.

options.content: Venous end: net pressure 1.7 kPa, promoting reabsorption.
options.markscheme: Incorrect value. Reabsorption occurs at the venous end, but the net pressure magnitude is $2.9 - 1.2 = 1.7\text{ kPa}$ (equivalently $-1.7\text{ kPa}$ as hydrostatic − osmotic).

| Condition        | Rate of Water Transport (mL h⁻¹) |
|-----------------|----------------------------------|
| Low Humidity    | 15.2                             |
| High Humidity   | 5.8                              |
| High Temperature | 20.5                             |

labeledSubSubTopics.path: B - Form and Function > B3.2 Transport > B3.2.7 Transport of water from roots to leaves during transpiration
labeledSubSubTopics.title: B3.2.7 Transport of water from roots to leaves during transpiration
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question is about rates of water transport through xylem under varying humidity and temperature, i.e., transpiration-driven transport of water from roots to leaves.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: B - Form and Function > B3.1 Gas exchange > B3.1.9 Transpiration as a consequence of gas exchange in a leaf
labeledSubSubTopics.title: B3.1.9 Transpiration as a consequence of gas exchange in a leaf
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Humidity and temperature effects are explained via transpiration (water loss during gas exchange in leaves) which drives xylem flow; the question implicitly tests transpiration rate changes with environmental conditions.

parts.content: Describe the trend in water transport rates under different conditions.
parts.markscheme:
- Water transport is highest in high temperature (20.5 mL h⁻¹) and lowest in low temperature (4.3 mL h⁻¹). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Low humidity (15.2 mL h⁻¹) results in higher transport rates than high humidity (5.8 mL h⁻¹). <marks>1 mark</marks>

_**OR**_

- Any correct comparative statement describing both a temperature effect (higher temperature → higher rate; lower temperature → lower rate) and a humidity effect (lower humidity → higher rate; higher humidity → lower rate). <marks>2 marks</marks>

parts.content: Explain why water transport is highest at high temperature and lowest at low temperature.
parts.markscheme:
- At high temperatures, transpiration increases because evaporation from leaf surfaces/mesophyll cell walls is faster, increasing transpiration pull (cohesion-tension) in the xylem. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- At low temperatures, evaporation and diffusion of water vapour are reduced (lower kinetic energy), so transpiration pull is reduced and water transport slows. <marks>1 mark</marks>

Additional acceptable points (credit as alternatives to either marking point above):
- Higher temperature lowers water potential of the air spaces/leaf, increasing the water potential gradient from xylem to air.
- Higher temperature increases the diffusion gradient for water vapour between leaf and external air.
- Lower temperature reduces stomatal opening (or increases closure), reducing transpiration.
- Increased viscosity of water at low temperature can reduce flow rate in xylem.

parts.content: Explain the importance of osmolarity in medical procedures and how the movement of ions via facilitated diffusion differs from their movement via active transport.
parts.markscheme:
- Osmolarity is the total concentration of solute particles in a solution; <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Tissues/organs for transplant must be kept in isotonic solutions; <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Isotonic solutions have the same osmolarity as the cytoplasm of the cells; <marks>1 mark</marks>
- This prevents the net movement of water by osmosis; <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Prevents cells from shriveling/crenation (in hypertonic) or swelling/bursting/lysis (in hypotonic); <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Facilitated diffusion is a passive process/does not require ATP; <marks>1 mark</marks>
- It moves ions along the concentration gradient (from high to low); <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Active transport requires energy/ATP; <marks>1 mark</marks>
- It moves ions against the concentration gradient (from low to high); <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Both involve the use of specific proteins (channels vs pumps); <marks>1 mark</marks>

_Award [4 max] if only osmolarity or only transport mechanisms are explained._

parts.content: Explain how electrical impulses regulate the heartbeat.
parts.markscheme:
- SA node initiates electrical impulses in right atrium (acts as pacemaker) <marks>1 mark</marks>

- SA node signals cause atrial contraction <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Impulses travel to AV node located between atria and ventricles <marks>1 mark</marks>

- AV node delays impulse allowing ventricles to fill with blood <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Impulses travel down bundle of His <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Bundle of His divides into right and left bundle branches <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Impulses reach Purkinje fibers throughout ventricles <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Coordinated contraction of atria and ventricles enables efficient blood pumping <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Award marks for correct sequence of events and clear understanding of the conduction system's role</note>

steps.title: Initiation at the SA Node
steps.content:
The process begins in the **Sinoatrial (SA) node**, which is a specialized group of cells located in the wall of the right atrium. This node acts as the heart's natural **pacemaker** by spontaneously generating electrical impulses without needing a signal from the brain (this property is known as being *myogenic*).

This first impulse sets the rhythm for the entire heart cycle.

highlights.text: SA node initiates electrical impulses in right atrium (acts as pacemaker)

steps.title: Atrial Contraction
steps.content:
Once the SA node fires, the electrical signal spreads rapidly through the muscle walls of both the left and right atria. 

This electrical stimulation causes the **atria to contract** simultaneously (atrial systole), which pushes blood through the open valves into the ventricles below.

highlights.text: SA node signals cause atrial contraction

steps.title: Arrival at the AV Node
steps.content:
The electrical impulse reaches a second "gateway" called the **Atrioventricular (AV) node**, located in the lower part of the right atrium near the border with the ventricles.

A crucial event happens here: the AV node **delays the impulse** for about 0.1 seconds. This pause is vital because it ensures the atria have completely finished contracting and the ventricles are fully filled with blood before they start to squeeze.

highlights.text: Impulses travel to AV node located between atria and ventricles
AV node delays impulse allowing ventricles to fill with blood

steps.title: Transmission through the Septum
steps.content:
After the short delay, the impulse travels down the **Bundle of His**. This is a bundle of specialized conducting fibers located in the septum (the wall separating the left and right sides of the heart).

The Bundle of His quickly divides into **right and left bundle branches**, which carry the signal down toward the apex (the very bottom) of the heart.

highlights.text: Impulses travel down bundle of His
Bundle of His divides into right and left bundle branches

steps.title: Ventricular Contraction
steps.content:
From the bundle branches, the signal enters the **Purkinje fibers**. These fibers spread upward through the muscle walls of the ventricles like the roots of a tree.

When the impulse reaches these fibers, it triggers the **ventricles to contract** (ventricular systole). Because the signal reaches the bottom of the heart first, the contraction begins at the apex and moves upward, efficiently squeezing blood out of the heart and into the arteries (the pulmonary artery and the aorta).

highlights.text: Impulses reach Purkinje fibers throughout ventricles

steps.title: Coordinated Blood Flow
steps.content:
The strict sequence of this electrical pathway—starting at the top and finishing at the bottom with a precise delay in the middle—is what allows for a **coordinated heartbeat**.

This ensures that the chambers work in harmony, maximizing the amount of blood pumped to the lungs and the rest of the body with every beat.

highlights.text: Coordinated contraction of atria and ventricles enables efficient blood pumping

steps.title: Identify the Conduction System
steps.content: To understand how the heartbeat is regulated, we use a **Structure-Function Mapping** approach. The heart is *myogenic*, meaning it generates its own electrical impulses. These impulses are controlled by a specialized pathway called the cardiac conduction system. This process is covered in **Topic B3.2: Transport**, specifically relating to the stages of the cardiac cycle.

steps.title: SA Node: The Pacemaker
steps.content:
**Structure:** The **Sinoatrial (SA) node** is a cluster of specialized cells located in the wall of the right atrium.

**Function:** It acts as the heart's primary **pacemaker**. It spontaneously initiates electrical impulses that spread across the walls of the atria, causing them to contract (atrial systole) and push blood into the ventricles.

highlights.text: SA node initiates electrical impulses in right atrium (acts as pacemaker)
SA node signals cause atrial contraction

steps.title: AV Node: The Delay
steps.content:
**Structure:** The impulse travels from the atria to the **Atrioventricular (AV) node**, situated in the floor of the right atrium near the septum.

**Function:** The AV node **delays** the impulse for approximately 0.1 seconds. This delay is critical because it ensures that the atria have finished contracting and the ventricles are fully filled with blood before they begin their own contraction.

highlights.text: Impulses travel to AV node located between atria and ventricles
AV node delays impulse allowing ventricles to fill with blood

steps.title: Bundle of His: Distribution
steps.content:
**Structure:** After the delay, the signal passes into the **Bundle of His** (or atrioventricular bundle) located in the interventricular septum.

**Function:** This bundle divides into the **right and left bundle branches**, which rapidly conduct the electrical signal down towards the apex (the bottom) of the heart.

highlights.text: Impulses travel down bundle of His
Bundle of His divides into right and left bundle branches

steps.title: Purkinje Fibers: Ventricular Contraction
steps.content:
**Structure:** The bundle branches lead to the **Purkinje fibers**, which are large-diameter conductive fibers that spread throughout the ventricular myocardium (the muscle walls).

**Function:** These fibers trigger the coordinated contraction of the ventricles (ventricular systole) starting from the apex and moving upward, efficiently squeezing blood out into the arteries (aorta and pulmonary artery).

highlights.text: Impulses reach Purkinje fibers throughout ventricles

steps.title: Efficiency through Coordination
steps.content: By mapping these specific structures to their functions, we see how the heart maintains a steady, rhythmic cycle. This precise sequence—starting at the atria and finishing at the ventricles after a controlled delay—enables the most **efficient pumping of blood** throughout the body.

highlights.text: Coordinated contraction of atria and ventricles enables efficient blood pumping

parts.content: Describe the changes in the Earth’s atmosphere and oceans resulting from the evolution of photosynthesis.
parts.markscheme:
- The first photosynthetic organisms were prokaryotes/cyanobacteria <marks>1 mark</marks>
- They produced oxygen ($O_2$) through the photolysis of water <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Oxygen reacted with dissolved iron in the oceans, causing it to precipitate as iron oxide <marks>1 mark</marks>
- This led to the formation of banded iron formations in sedimentary rocks <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Atmospheric oxygen concentrations rose significantly from trace amounts/near zero to approximately $20\%$ <marks>1 mark</marks>
- This oxygenation allowed for the development of the ozone layer

_**OR**_

- This oxygenation allowed for the evolution of aerobic respiration <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Outline how chromatography is used to separate and identify photosynthetic pigments.
parts.markscheme:
- Leaf tissue is ground with a solvent (e.g., acetone or propanone) to extract pigments <marks>1 mark</marks>
- A concentrated spot of the extract is placed on the baseline/origin of the chromatography paper or TLC plate <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The paper/plate is placed in a container with a running solvent <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Pigments travel at different speeds based on their solubility in the solvent/affinity for the paper <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The distance moved by the pigment is compared to the solvent front to calculate the $Rf$ value <marks>1 mark</marks>
- $Rf = \frac{\text{distance moved by pigment}}{\text{distance moved by solvent front}}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain how temperature, light intensity, and carbon dioxide concentration act as limiting factors of photosynthesis.
parts.markscheme:
- A limiting factor is the factor furthest from its optimum that restricts the rate of a reaction <marks>1 mark</marks>
- As light intensity increases, the rate increases up to a plateau <marks>1 mark</marks>
- At the plateau, light is no longer limiting and another factor (e.g., $CO_2$) is in short supply <marks>1 mark</marks>
- As $CO_2$ concentration increases, the rate increases up to a plateau <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Temperature increases the rate up to an optimum due to increased molecular collisions <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Beyond the optimum temperature, enzymes (e.g., Rubisco) denature and the rate drops sharply <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The rate of photosynthesis can be measured directly by oxygen ($O_2$) production/bubble counting <marks>1 mark</marks>
- ...or by the rate of carbon dioxide ($CO_2$) uptake/pH change in water <marks>1 mark</marks>
- ...or indirectly by the increase in dry biomass of the plant <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Up to one additional mark for an accurate detail from AHL (e.g., mention of the light-independent stage or carboxylation)</note>

A new experiment investigates how surface acoustic waves (SAW) can increase transpiration in a cut leaf. In the setup shown, a cut leaf is placed on a device that applies mechanical waves (SAW) to a small area of the leaf. The leaf is submerged in a blue dye solution. The dye helps visualize water movement through the xylem and evaporation from the surface.

Figure A:

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/cIee82ZawG2OJrCLbBWSw.jpeg)

Diagram of the SAW setup, with panels showing normal transpiration without SAW applied and increased water evaporation and dye movement when SAW is applied.

Figure B:

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/hvGkvZjaEcmX7_XFHGSRr.jpeg)

Photograph of the SAW device and leaf images over time, showing dye accumulation at the treated area at 0, 10, 30, and 240 minutes.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: B - Form and Function > B3.2 Transport > B3.2.7 Transport of water from roots to leaves during transpiration
labeledSubSubTopics.title: B3.2.7 Transport of water from roots to leaves during transpiration
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Core of the question is transpiration-driven water movement through xylem into the leaf (dye tracking), including explaining why dye moves during transpiration and how tension pulls water through xylem.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: B - Form and Function > B3.2 Transport > B3.2.8 Adaptations of xylem vessels for transport of water
labeledSubSubTopics.title: B3.2.8 Adaptations of xylem vessels for transport of water
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Includes a part asking for a feature/adaptation of xylem vessels supporting water transport.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: B - Form and Function > B3.1 Gas exchange > B3.1.9 Transpiration as a consequence of gas exchange in a leaf
labeledSubSubTopics.title: B3.1.9 Transpiration as a consequence of gas exchange in a leaf
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Transpiration itself (evaporation from leaf surface linked to stomata/gas exchange) is central to interpreting the figures and explaining the mechanism generating tension.

parts.content: Using Figures A and B, describe how SAW affects dye movement through the leaf.
parts.markscheme:
- Dye movement is faster and/or more concentrated in the SAW-treated region. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Dye accumulates visibly at the treated region by 30–240 minutes (compared with earlier times). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Dye staining spreads out from/around the treated point over time as more dyed water is pulled through nearby veins. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Little/no comparable dye accumulation is seen in untreated regions over the same time period. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain why the dye moves from the stem into the leaf during transpiration.
parts.markscheme:
- Water (with dissolved dye) moves through xylem vessels from stem to leaf as part of the transpiration stream. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Evaporation of water from mesophyll cell walls and diffusion of water vapour out through stomata lowers water potential in the leaf. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- This creates negative pressure/tension in the xylem that pulls water upward (cohesion–tension mechanism). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Cohesion between water molecules and adhesion to xylem walls maintain a continuous water column. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Suggest a reason why dye accumulates in the region treated with SAW but not elsewhere on the leaf.
parts.markscheme:
- SAW increases the local rate of evaporation, increasing transpiration at that spot and drawing in more dyed water to that region. <marks>1 mark</marks>
_**OR**_
- SAW increases stomatal opening and/or disrupts the boundary layer locally, increasing water loss and so increasing local uptake of dyed water. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: From the figures, state the earliest time at which the effect of SAW becomes clearly visible.
parts.markscheme:
- 10 minutes. <marks>1 mark</marks>
_**OR**_
- 30 minutes. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: State one feature of xylem vessels that supports their function in water transport.
parts.markscheme:
- No end walls / continuous hollow tube for uninterrupted flow. <marks>1 mark</marks>
_**OR**_
- Thick, lignified walls to resist collapse under tension. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Outline how transpiration generates tension to pull water through the xylem.
parts.markscheme:
- Water evaporates from the leaf (from mesophyll cell walls) and exits via stomata, lowering water potential in the leaf. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- This creates negative pressure/tension in the xylem that pulls water upward (transpiration pull). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Cohesion between water molecules maintains a continuous water column so the pull is transmitted down the xylem. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Adhesion of water to xylem walls helps support the column against gravity. <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification:
The graph below shows the changes in heart rate of an individual before, during, and after a period of intense exercise.

![graph](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/ramen-ramen-ib-physics-new-ee258bd2-601b-475c-a5dc-cf8591aa1165-1771929956947/c5023ff3-f226-41a9-8581-4d6ac8519f39.jpeg)

Which physiological mechanism is primarily responsible for the trend observed between minute 40 and minute 55?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C - Interaction and Interdependence > C3.1 Integration of body systems > C3.1.14 Feedback control of heart rate
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C3.1.14 Feedback control of heart rate
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks which physiological mechanism explains the decrease in heart rate after exercise (minute 40–55), which is controlled by autonomic negative feedback acting on the sinoatrial node (e.g., increased vagal input).

options.content: Increased frequency of impulses sent via the vagus nerve to the sinoatrial node
Increased release of epinephrine from the adrenal glands into the bloodstream
A higher frequency of nerve impulses arriving from the sympathetic nervous system
Direct detection of reduced carbon dioxide levels by the heart muscle in the right ventricle

questions.specification:
The table describes the status of heart valves during a specific phase of the cardiac cycle.

| | Atrioventricular (AV) valves | Semi-lunar (SL) valves |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **A** | Open | Closed |
| **B** | Closed | Open |
| **C** | Closed | Closed |
| **D** | Open | Open |

Which row shows valve status at the start of ventricular systole (isovolumetric contraction), when ventricular pressure is rising but still below aortic and pulmonary artery pressure?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: B - Form and Function > B3.2 Transport > B3.2.16 Stages in the cardiac cycle (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: B3.2.16 Stages in the cardiac cycle (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks about the onset of ventricular systole and which valves (AV vs semilunar) are open/closed during this cardiac cycle phase (isovolumetric contraction), which is directly part of the stages in the cardiac cycle.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: B - Form and Function > B3.2 Transport > B3.2.15 Adaptations of the mammalian heart for delivering pressurized blood to the arteries (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: B3.2.15 Adaptations of the mammalian heart for delivering pressurized blood to the arteries (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: It touches on how the heart generates pressure and valve states as part of delivering pressurized blood, which relates to heart adaptations for pressurized flow, though the primary focus is the cardiac cycle stage.

options.content: A
options.markscheme: Incorrect. This describes atrial systole or the end of diastole, where blood is flowing into the ventricles.

options.content: B
options.markscheme: Incorrect. This describes the ejection phase of ventricular systole, which occurs only after ventricular pressure exceeds arterial pressure.

options.content: C
options.markscheme: Correct. During isovolumetric contraction (the start of systole), the AV valves snap shut due to rising pressure, but the semi-lunar valves remain closed until the pressure is high enough to force them open.

options.content: D
options.markscheme: Incorrect. There is no phase in a healthy cardiac cycle where all four valves are open simultaneously.

questions.specification:
The diagram below shows a transverse section of a young dicotyledonous root.

![diagram](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/ramen-ramen-ib-biology-new-f20d8fe0-d72b-42b6-8b0b-df9c0de7ad0c-1771815806714/c160e406-ef90-46cc-874b-cb3c12c6cd59.jpeg)

Which letter indicates the tissue primarily responsible for the translocation of organic solutes, such as sucrose and amino acids, to the root cells?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: B - Form and Function > B3.2 Transport > B3.2.10 Distribution of tissues in a transverse section of the root of a dicotyledonous plant
labeledSubSubTopics.title: B3.2.10 Distribution of tissues in a transverse section of the root of a dicotyledonous plant
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks which labelled tissue in a transverse section of a young dicot root is responsible for translocation of organic solutes (phloem), i.e., identifying tissues in a dicot root cross-section.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: B - Form and Function > B3.2 Transport > B3.2.18 Adaptations of phloem sieve tubes and companion cells for translocation of sap (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: B3.2.18 Adaptations of phloem sieve tubes and companion cells for translocation of sap (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: It specifically targets phloem function (translocation of sucrose/amino acids) and recognition of phloem (sieve tubes/companion cells) as the transport tissue for organic solutes.

options.content: A
options.markscheme: A indicates the endodermis (inner boundary of the cortex), not phloem.

options.content: B
options.markscheme: B indicates the epidermis, the outermost protective layer.

options.content: C
options.markscheme: C indicates phloem, which translocates organic solutes such as sucrose and amino acids.

options.content: D
options.markscheme: D indicates xylem, which transports water and dissolved mineral ions.

questions.specification:
The table shows the target and maximum heart rates for individuals of different ages. The target range represents 50-85% of maximum heart rate. These values are used for fat-burning exercises.

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/4d71a8c1-8df6-4b5e-b9a7-533034439e91)

labeledSubSubTopics.path: B - Form and Function > B3.2 Transport > B3.2.4 Measurement of pulse rates
labeledSubSubTopics.title: B3.2.4 Measurement of pulse rates
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Question focuses on interpreting a table of target/max heart rates by age, checking whether a measured pulse lies within a fat-burning target range, and discussing pros/cons of using heart-rate targets—directly aligned with measurement and use of pulse rates.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C - Interaction and Interdependence > C3.1 Integration of body systems > C3.1.14 Feedback control of heart rate
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C3.1.14 Feedback control of heart rate
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Touches on how heart rate changes with age and exercise recommendations; this loosely relates to control/feedback of heart rate, though the question is more data-interpretation than physiological control mechanisms.

parts.content: Identify the age group with the widest target heart rate range. Calculate the width of this range in beats per minute (bpm).
parts.markscheme:
- Identifies 20 years as having the widest target heart-rate range <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Uses the correct method: upper limit minus lower limit (e.g. $170-100$) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Calculates the width as 70 bpm <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Accept equivalent justification by comparing widths across age groups (e.g. 30 years: $162-95=67$; 70 years: $128-75=53$) <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Outline the trend observed in maximum heart rate as age increases.
parts.markscheme:
- States that maximum heart rate decreases as age increases <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Accept equivalent wording such as “maximum heart rate declines with increasing age” <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content:
A 40-year-old measured their pulse as 175 bpm after exercise using a digital wrist monitor.

Explain whether this value falls within the fat-burning target range and how such values can inform exercise recommendations.
parts.markscheme:
- States that 175 bpm is above the fat-burning target range for a 40-year-old (90–153 bpm), so it is not within the target zone <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Supports with comparison to the upper limit: $175>153$ <marks>1 mark</marks>
- States that 175 bpm is close to/near the maximum heart rate for a 40-year-old (180 bpm) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Explains how it informs recommendations: intensity should be reduced to bring heart rate into the target zone (or exercise prescription can be adjusted using heart-rate zones to meet training goals safely) <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Suggest one advantage and one limitation of using heart rate targets to guide fat-burning exercise plans.
parts.markscheme:
Advantage (any one):
- Provides a measurable target to monitor and adjust exercise intensity <marks>1 mark</marks>
_**OR**_
- Helps maintain a safe intensity by avoiding excessively high heart rates <marks>1 mark</marks>
_**OR**_
- Helps keep exercise within a chosen training zone (e.g. “fat-burning” zone) <marks>1 mark</marks>

Limitation (any one):
- Does not account for individual variation (e.g. fitness level, medication, health conditions) <marks>1 mark</marks>
_**OR**_
- Heart-rate monitors/measurements can be inaccurate (device error, movement artefacts) <marks>1 mark</marks>
_**OR**_
- Heart rate is affected by other factors (e.g. temperature, stress, hydration), so targets may not reflect effort precisely <marks>1 mark</marks>

labeledSubSubTopics.title: B4.2.4 Holozoic nutrition in animals
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question focuses on identifying organs within the human digestive system and their specific roles (secreting HCl for digestion), which is a core component of holozoic nutrition in animals.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C - Interaction and Interdependence > C1.1 Enzymes and metabolism > C1.1.8 Effects of temperature, pH and substrate concentration on the rate of enzyme activity
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C1.1.8 Effects of temperature, pH and substrate concentration on the rate of enzyme activity

options.content: A

options.content: B
options.markscheme: <note>B is the stomach, which secretes hydrochloric acid and pepsin.</note>

options.content: C
options.markscheme: <note>C is the pancreas, which produces alkaline pancreatic juice containing various enzymes.</note>

options.content: D
options.markscheme: <note>D is the small intestine, where final digestion and absorption occur.</note>

questions.specification:
Which of the following correctly describes the blood as it returns to the heart via the pulmonary vein?

| Enters | Oxygenation status |
| :--- | :--- |
| right atrium | oxygenated |
| right atrium | deoxygenated |
| left atrium | oxygenated |
| left atrium | deoxygenated |

options.content: Enters right atrium; oxygenated
options.markscheme: Incorrect. Pulmonary veins return blood to the left atrium, not the right atrium.

options.content: Enters right atrium; deoxygenated
options.markscheme: Incorrect. Deoxygenated blood returns to the right atrium via the vena cava, not via the pulmonary vein.

options.content: Enters left atrium; oxygenated
options.markscheme: Correct. Pulmonary veins carry oxygenated blood from the lungs to the left atrium.

options.content: Enters left atrium; deoxygenated
options.markscheme: Incorrect. Blood in the pulmonary vein is oxygenated after gas exchange in the lungs.

questions.specification:
The table shows results from a potometer investigation of transpiration rate in a leafy shoot under different environmental conditions.

| Experimental Setup | Environmental Conditions | Mean distance moved by the air bubble in 10 minutes (mm) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| I | Still air at $22^\circ\text{C}$ | 18 |
| II | Moving air (fan) at $22^\circ\text{C}$ | 41 |
| III | Still air at $22^\circ\text{C}$ with a mist spray | 7 |
| IV | Still air at $12^\circ\text{C}$ | 11 |

Which of the following is a valid conclusion based on the data provided?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: B - Form and Function > B3.2 Transport > B3.2.7 Transport of water from roots to leaves during transpiration
labeledSubSubTopics.title: B3.2.7 Transport of water from roots to leaves during transpiration
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question describes a potometer experiment, which is the standard tool for measuring the rate of transpiration, a key process in the transport of water from roots to leaves.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: B - Form and Function > B3.1 Gas exchange > B3.1.9 Transpiration as a consequence of gas exchange in a leaf
labeledSubSubTopics.title: B3.1.9 Transpiration as a consequence of gas exchange in a leaf
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The experiment investigates how environmental conditions (temperature, humidity, and wind) affect the rate of transpiration, which is fundamentally linked to gas exchange through the stomata.

options.content: A fan in Setup II decreased transpiration by increasing the boundary layer of water vapour.
options.markscheme: Incorrect. Setup II shows a higher air-bubble distance (41 mm) than Setup I (18 mm), indicating increased transpiration with air movement. Air movement decreases the boundary layer, not increases it.

options.content: The lower temperature in Setup IV decreased transpiration by reducing kinetic energy of water molecules.
options.markscheme: Correct. Setup IV (12°C) shows a smaller air-bubble distance (11 mm) than Setup I (22°C, 18 mm), consistent with lower temperature reducing evaporation/transpiration due to reduced kinetic energy.

options.content: The mist spray in Setup III increased the water potential gradient between the leaf and the atmosphere.
options.markscheme: Incorrect. Mist increases humidity, reducing the water potential gradient, which is consistent with the reduced air-bubble distance in Setup III (7 mm).

options.content: Air movement has a smaller impact on water loss than changes in ambient temperature.
options.markscheme: Incorrect. Air movement increases distance from 18 mm to 41 mm (+23 mm), whereas temperature change decreases from 18 mm to 11 mm (-7 mm), so air movement has the larger impact here.

questions.specification:
A mature leaf of a herbaceous plant is supplied with radioactive carbon dioxide ($^{14}CO_2$). Several hours later, a transverse section of the stem is prepared and analysed to track the movement of the radioactive carbon.

![diagram](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/ramen-ramen-ib-chemistry-new-fe5cb0ba-3b2d-4143-ae9a-31f92322de9f-1771866504453/3fd56ce6-4927-4726-aed6-232cd608abe7.jpeg)

In which labelled region of the stem would the radioactivity be most concentrated, and what is the primary substance being transported in that tissue?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: B - Form and Function > B3.2 Transport > B3.2.9 Distribution of tissues in a transverse section of the stem of a dicotyledonous plant
labeledSubSubTopics.title: B3.2.9 Distribution of tissues in a transverse section of the stem of a dicotyledonous plant
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question requires identifying tissues (phloem and xylem) within a transverse section of a dicotyledonous stem based on a provided diagram.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: B - Form and Function > B3.2 Transport > B3.2.18 Adaptations of phloem sieve tubes and companion cells for translocation of sap (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: B3.2.18 Adaptations of phloem sieve tubes and companion cells for translocation of sap (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question involves the translocation of organic compounds (sucrose) through the phloem tissue, which is the process being tracked with radioactive carbon.

options.content: Region P; Sucrose
options.markscheme: Region P represents the phloem, located on the outer part of the vascular bundle. Phloem is the specialised tissue for the translocation of organic solutes, such as sucrose, which would incorporate the radioactive $^{14}C$ during photosynthesis.

options.content: Region Q; Mineral ions
options.markscheme: Region Q represents the xylem, located on the inner part of the vascular bundle. Xylem is specialised for the transport of water and dissolved mineral ions, not the products of photosynthesis.

options.content: Region R; Starch
options.markscheme: Region R represents the cortex. While it may store starch, it is not a specialised tissue for the long-distance transport of radioactive sucrose throughout the plant.

options.content: Region P; Water
options.markscheme: While the phloem (Region P) contains water as a solvent, its primary and specialised transport function investigated by tracking radioactive carbon is the movement of sucrose.
